bodo_festivals.name: Bwisagu
bodo_festivals.description: Bwisagu is the most important traditional festival of the Bodo people, celebrated as the Bodo New Year. It marks the beginning of the new year according to the Bodo calendar and is celebrated with great enthusiasm and traditional fervor. The festival usually falls in the month of April (mid-April) and is celebrated over several days. Bwisagu is a time for family reunions, cultural performances, and traditional rituals that reflect the agricultural heritage of the Bodo people.
bodo_festivals.cultural_significance: Bwisagu represents the renewal of life and the agricultural cycle. It is a time when the Bodo community comes together to celebrate their cultural heritage and strengthen social bonds. The festival reflects the deep connection between the Bodo people and nature, as it marks the spring harvest season.

bodo_festivals.name: Kherai
bodo_festivals.description: Kherai is the most important religious festival of the Bodo people, celebrated in honor of the deity 'Bathou' (the supreme god in Bodo religion). It is a week-long festival that typically falls in the months of November-December. During Kherai, the Bodo people offer prayers to Bathou and other deities for prosperity, good health, and bumper harvests. The festival involves elaborate rituals performed by the 'Deodhai' (priest) and 'Yajman' (sponsor of the ritual).
rituals: Cleaning of the 'Ghor Chang Ghar' (ritual hut)
Performance of traditional dances by priest
bodo_festivals.cultural_significance: Kherai is central to the religious identity of the Bodo people as it reinforces their indigenous faith in Bathouism. The festival showcases the unique religious traditions and rituals of the Bodo community that have been preserved for centuries. It serves as a means of transmitting religious knowledge and cultural practices to younger generations.

bodo_festivals.name: Garja
bodo_festivals.description: Garja is a traditional agricultural festival celebrated by the Bodo people after the harvest is completed. It is a way of expressing gratitude to the gods for a successful harvest. The festival is characterized by traditional songs, dances, and feasting. Garja is particularly important for farmers as it marks the end of the agricultural year and the beginning of a period of rest and celebration.
bodo_festivals.cultural_significance: Garja reflects the agricultural roots of the Bodo people and their close relationship with nature. The festival demonstrates the community's gratitude for the bounty of nature and reinforces the importance of agriculture in Bodo society.

bodo_festivals.name: Domashi
bodo_festivals.description: Domashi is another important harvest festival celebrated by the Bodo people, typically in the month of March. It marks the harvesting of the winter crops and is a time of joy and celebration. Domashi is closely associated with the agricultural cycle and is celebrated with traditional songs, dances, and feasting.
rituals: Traditional cooking of new harvest produce
Offering prayers for next season's harvest
bodo_festivals.cultural_significance: Domashi represents the prosperity and abundance of the Bodo agricultural society. It is a time when the community comes together to celebrate the fruits of their labor and pray for future prosperity.

bodo_festivals.name: Sankranti
bodo_festivals.description: Sankranti is an important seasonal festival celebrated by the Bodo people, marking the transition from one season to another. It is particularly important as it marks the winter solstice and the beginning of longer days. The festival is characterized by traditional foods, kite flying, and community gatherings.
bodo_festivals.cultural_significance: Sankranti celebrates the changing of seasons and the cyclical nature of time in agricultural societies. It reflects the Bodo people's awareness of astronomical phenomena and their integration with natural cycles.

bodo_festivals.name: Aathar
bodo_festivals.description: Aathar is a traditional Bodo festival dedicated to the worship of ancestors and spirits. It is observed as a time to honor and remember departed ancestors. The festival involves rituals performed by the family elder to ensure the peaceful journey of ancestors and to seek their blessings.
bodo_festivals.cultural_significance: Aathar reflects the importance of ancestral worship in Bodo culture. It demonstrates the strong family ties and respect for traditions that characterize Bodo society.

bodo_festivals.name: Nwandw Khrung Khrung
bodo_festivals.description: Nwandw Khrung Khrung is a unique Bodo festival that celebrates the completion of the sowing season. It is a prayers for a good harvest and is celebrated with traditional songs and dances. The festival brings together the community to celebrate the agricultural cycle and seek divine blessings for a bountiful harvest.
bodo_festivals.cultural_significance: This festival represents the hope and aspirations of farmers for a successful agricultural season. It reinforces the community's connection to the land and their reliance on natural cycles.
